coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nanosensors based on green carbon dots (CDs) and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) for environmental pollutants: Emerging trends and future prospects

coredata.dc:description: Extraordinary features of carbon dots (CDs) including their biocompatibility, water solubility, stability, tunability, low cost and scalability make them as promising nanomaterials for a number of application areas such as fluorescent nanosensors. Combination of these superiorities with the advantages of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) such as high affinity, selectivity, robustness and reusability provides the development of novel fluorescent nanosensors with enhanced sensitivity and selectivity towards the target compounds. This paper provides an overview of green approaches for the synthesis of CDs, integration of CDs with MIPs and recent developments on the design and construction of sensitive green CDs/MIPs-based fluorescent nanosensors for the detection of various pollutants (i.e. antibiotics, pesticides, heavy metals and explosive compounds etc.) in environmental samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nanosensors based on green carbon dots (CDs) and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) for environmental pollutants: Emerging trends and future prospects

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Extraordinary features of carbon dots (CDs) including their biocompatibility, water solubility, stability, tunability, low cost and scalability make them as promising nanomaterials for a number of application areas such as fluorescent nanosensors. Combination of these superiorities with the advantages of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) such as high affinity, selectivity, robustness and reusability provides the development of novel fluorescent nanosensors with enhanced sensitivity and selectivity towards the target compounds. This paper provides an overview of green approaches for the synthesis of CDs, integration of CDs with MIPs and recent developments on the design and construction of sensitive green CDs/MIPs-based fluorescent nanosensors for the detection of various pollutants (i.e. antibiotics, pesticides, heavy metals and explosive compounds etc.) in environmental samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), also known as “carbon quantum dots” are a type of carbon-based nanomaterials which display superior optical and electronic features [1,2]. They are nanoscale carbon particles having sizes typically less than 10 nm. These unique nanomaterials were first discovered in 2004 during the purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes [3]. CDs have a diverse range of features (i.e. excellent optical features, biocompatibility and ecofriendliness etc.) [4] which make them attractive for a number of applications including drug relase [5], sensing [6], catalysis [7], bioimaging [8] and photodynamic therapy [9]. Sensing processes using CDs are based on the quenching mechanism of CDs in a fluorescent sensor systems. The quenching mechanism involves the reduction of the fluorescence intensity of CDs due to the presence of specific analytes. It has been reported that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is be mainly divided into two categories. One of these categories is static quenching and the other one is dynamic quenching. Static quenching process involves the formation of a ground-state complex between the analyte and the CDs. The formation of this complex prevents the excited-state transition necessary for fluorescence emission, thus leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. This interaction could be due to factors like electron transfer, charge transfer, or energy transfer between the carbon dots and the analyte. In dynamic quenching process, the analyte interacts with the CDs in a collisional manner. The excited-state carbon dots transfer energy to the analyte through a non-radiative pathway, thereby reducing the fluorescence emission. If static quenching occurs, there may be a formation of a binary complex and the lifetime of fluorescence molecule on excited state has no change in the existence of quencher. However, when dynamic quenching process takes place, the fluorescence lifetime become shorter [10].

On the other hand, molecular imprinting is a powerful technique which can be successfully utilized to design and prepare functional and highly cross-linked molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) that are synthetic materials exhibit great recognition features towards specific target compound/s [11,12]. MIPs have several advantages that make them attractive materials for various applications such sensing [13,14]. extraction [15–19]. drug relase [20]. catalysis [21,22] and bioimaging [23]. These advantages include high selectivity, great affinity for the target compound, easy preparation, low cost, robustness and tailored design etc. Robustness is one of the crucial properties of MIPs because it affects their practical utilization in different applications. In the context of MIPs, robustness refers to the ability of the polymer to maintain its structural integrity and recognition performance towards the target compound under various conditions, including changes in pH, temperature, solvent composition etc [24]. On the other hand, tailored design for MIPs refers to a systematic approach where the molecular imprinting process is carefully customized and optimized to achieve specific desired properties and functionalities in the resulting polymer. This approach involves designing MIPs with precision to meet the requirements of a particular application or target molecule The goal of this approach is to enhance the selectivity, sensitivity, and overall performance of the MIP for its intended purpose [25].

The synthesis of both CDs and MIPs considering the green chemistry principles is very crucial. The goal of the design and development of green approaches for the synthesis of CDs and MIPs is to decrease use of hazardous chemicals, energy consumption, waste generation, long reaction time, multistep processes and extreme conditions (i.e. high pressure and temperature). In 2021, Arabi et al. formulated a comprehensive set of 14 principles, known as “GREENIFICATION” [26] to complement the 12 established principles of green chemistry. The aim of these principles is to promote the design, preparation and application of MIPs in an environmentally-friendly and sustainable manner, The GREENIFICATION principles emphasize the importance of minimizing waste, energy, using renewable resources, and reducing the environmental impacts of MIPs throughout their life cycle [27].

Green composite nanomaterials based on MIPs and CDs combine the unique superiorities of both components to fabricate hybrid materials with enhanced functionalities and selectivity [28] These composites can be synthesized through incorporating CDs into the MIP matrix during the polymerization process or by post-modification of pre-formed MIPs with CDs. The resulting CDs-MIP composite materials exhibit several advantages including improved selectivity, enhanced sensing behavior and reusability that significantly reduce the process costs.

In this review paper, we present and discuss the latest achievements of fluorescence nanosensors based on green CDs and MIPs through highlighting the following points:
➣
Superiorities of CDs and MIPs.
➣
Importance of their combination for sensing applications.
➣
Approaches the preparation of green CDs.
➣
Environmental applications of fluorescence nanosensors composed of green CDs and MIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green strategies for the synthesis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Green strategies for the synthesis of CDs aim to reduce the use of hazardous chemicals, energy consumption, waste generation, long reaction time, multistep processes, extreme pressure and temperature.

There are 2 main strategies for the synthesis of green CDs which are “Bottom-Up” and “Top-Down” approaches. These green strategies, that can be pursued through facile operations, eco-friendly and low-cost approaches, are briefly described in the following:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of the Bottom-Up strategies for the synthesis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The bottom-up strategies involve the construction of CDs from molecular or atomic building blocks, gradually building up the carbon structure to form the desired nanoscale structures. These strategies typically use small organic molecules or precursors as the starting materials that are then subjected to various synthetic processes for the synthesis of CDs.

Hydrothermal method also called “hydrothermal carbonization approach” is one of the most commonly utilized green strategy for the efficient synthesis of CDs [29]. This method involves the use of water as a solvent and operates at moderate temperatures and pressures. It typically utilizes carbon sources such as biomass, waste materials, or carbon-rich precursors. The reaction occurs in a sealed vessel, where the carbon source is subjected to heat and pressure, leading to the formation of CDs. This approach has advantages including cost-effectiveness, eco-friendly nature and non-hazardous method. However, the main disadvantages of this method are low yield, uncontrolled size distribution of the obtained CDs and existence of various impurities [30].

In this strategy, microwave irradiation is applied to heat the reaction mixture, accelerating the synthesis of CDs [31]. The method consists of exposing green precursors such as extract of the material or partially dried and grinded contents to microwave irradiation via a microwave oven in the open air/closed container. The use of small reactors may limit the synthesis of CDs in large-scale. Despite the various difficulties encountered in terms of the control of particle size, the main superiority of this approach is its ease of accessibility, efficiency in terms of energy consumption and short reaction time [32].

Pyrolysis method is a thermal treatment process based on the decomposition of organic precursors in the absence of oxygen, leading to the formation of carbon-rich materials through applying temperature varies in the range between 160 and 1000 °C [33]. The pyrolysis method offers a versatile and scalable approach to synthesis CDs with controllable size, surface functionalization, and optical features. The applied low temperature values can prevent the complete carbonization of the material to CDs. However, the carbon source can be over-oxidized and the surface structure of CDs can possibly be altered to considerably impact optical performances of the produced CDs [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of Top-Down strategies for the synthesis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Top-Down strategies for the synthesis of CDs are electrochemical synthesis, laser ablation, chemical oxidation method, ultrasonic treatment method and arc discharge-based synthesis.

Electrochemical method for they synthesis of CDs involve the electrolysis of suitable carbon sources including organic molecules or biomass, in an electrolyte solution. The process utilizes electricity as an energy source and does not require high temperatures or hazardous chemicals. Electrochemical synthesis is an economical, environment-friendly and sustainable approach for the efficient synthesis of CDs with high purity and reproducibility [35]. However, it requires specific steps during the production process and specialized equipments, which may limit the possible scalability of CDs [36].

Laser ablation method is effectively applied for the synthesis of CDs via the process of laser-induced breakdown of a carbon target material [37]. It involves the irradiation of a carbon-based solid target with a high-intensity laser pulse, leading to the vaporization and fragmentation of the target material. The resulting plume contains vaporized carbon species that subsequently undergo condensation and recombination to form CDs with a relatively low quantum yield (4.5–18 %) [38]. The advantages of this method are controllable size, high purity of CDs and reproducibility while its disadvantages are high process cost, low quantum yield and limited large production.

Chemical oxidation method exhibits a number of superiorities including great selectivity, facile synthesis in large scale. In this strategy, conventional or biomass-derived carbon sources are treated/oxidized employing various oxidants such as H2O2.

Xue et al. reported the effective utilization of cheap and long-flame coal for the synthesis of green CDs via one-step ozone oxidation strategy [39]. In another research of Feng and Zhang [40], a chemical oxidation-based simple and green strategy using H2O2 was employed for the efficient synthesis of green CDs.

In this strategy, high-energy ultrasonic sound waves are applied to the carbon source that is enable to break down carbon molecules into the CDs [41]. The utilized ultrasonic sound in this strategy has higher energy compared to the traditional audible sound. Thus, this feature lead to fast collision of liquid flow and high shearing force of fluid which also provides efficient pyrolysis process, combustion and cleaving of the molecule bond in the aqueous medium [42]. Ultrasonic treatment method is a facile and low-cost approach for the production of CDs. However, its main disadvantage is the low production volume of the obtained CDs. In a work conducted by Park et al. [43], ultrasonic treatment method was effectively utilized for the synthesis of water-soluble green CDs. In this report, raw food was preferred as the carbon source. The synthesized green CDs displayed high photostability and low cytotoxicity which confirmes their great potential applications in bioimaging studies.

This method involves the utilization of high-voltage electric current to graphite electrodes that leads to the evaporation and subsequent condensation of carbon species to form carbon nanoparticles or clusters that can then further transform into CDs [44]. While arc discharge method is a feasible approach to produce CDs, its main disadvantages are large size distribution, complex operation and low yield [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green strategies for the preparation of MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The aim of the development of various green strategies for the preparation of MIPs is to decrease the undesired environmental effects of the preparation process through minimizing the use of hazardous chemicals and solvents, decrease the energy consumption, and improving the overall sustainability of the process [46]. Various green strategies for the preparation of MIPs are briefly described in the following.

Microwave assisted synthesis can be effectively utilized for the preparation of MIPs since it has display a number of superiorities including accelerated reaction rates, improved polymerization efficiency and reduced energy consumption, energy efficiency, reduced reaction time, solvent minimization, and improved yield [47]. By leveraging the energy efficiency, reduced reaction times, solvent minimization, and compatibility with green solvents, microwave-assisted synthesis offers a greener alternative for producing MIPs. It helps minimize the environmental footprint, conserve energy resources, and promote sustainable synthesis practices.

Supercritical fluid technology involves the employment of supercritical fluids such as carbon dioxide (CO2) as green solvent during the preparation of MIPs. This approach is an excellent option for the effective and green preparation of MIPs because of its environmentally-friendly features including minimal environmental impact, energy efficiency, enhanced mass transfer, recycling and green solvent properties [48].

Ultrasound-assisted preparation is another powerful, effective and green approach for the preparation of MIPs [49]. It exhibits many advantages including decreased amount of solvent usage, accelerated reaction kinetics and enhanced polymer morphology. Ultrasound waves lead to creation of micro streaming and micro turbulence within the reaction mixture. This agitation promotes the initiation and propagation of polymerization reactions, resulting in faster reaction kinetics. The enhanced mixing and improved collision frequency of reactive species under ultrasound irradiation led to shorter reaction times. Consequently, the energy consumption is reduced, making the synthesis process more efficient and sustainable.

On the other hand, scalability is another superiority of ultrasound-assisted preparation approach. This approach can be easily scaled up for industrial production. The process can be implemented in continuous flow systems, where the reactants are continuously fed into the reactor, and the product is continuously collected. Continuous flow systems exhibit a number of advantages such as improved efficiency effective control over the reaction parameters. This scalability and continuous operation contribute to more sustainable fabrication processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polymerization techniques for the preparation of green MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Various environmentally-friendly polymerization techniques such as green precipitation polymerization, green emulsion polymerization and green electrochemical polymerization are efficiently applied for the preparation of green MIPs. These techniques are presented in the following sections.

Precipitation polymerization is a straightforward and efficient approach for the preparation of uniform MIP particles at nano- and micro scales. Even without the presence of a stabilizer, the polymerization process can be easily activated utilizing diluted MIP reagents and the produced MIPs can be easily separated from the reaction solution in the shape of identical spherical polymer particles [50]. If this setup is to be used for preparation of MIPs on a big scale (above the kilogram), it is subject to substantial limitations. This method also consumes high porogen, which is another drawback that reduces its viability.

Emulsion polymerization is another commonly applied green approach for the preparation of MIP particles. It involves the polymerization of functional monomers dispersed in a water phase, typically using an emulsifying agent to stabilize the emulsion. This process provides uniform sphere-shaped polymers. Based on the size of the emulsified droplets, emulsions are categorized as macroemulsions, miniemulsions, or microemulsions. Decompte ad colleagues successfully applied 'green miniemulsion polymerization' in water-based dispersion media for the preparation of submicronic MIP colloids [51]. One of the green aspects of this approach is the use of water as the dispersion medium instead of toxic organic solvents. This polymerization technique also allows for precise control over particle size and distribution, leading to desired properties in the resulting MIPs. By adjusting the various parameters such as surfactant concentration and stirring speed, the particle size and distribution of the MIP particles can be easily controlled and optimized. This control enhances the performance of MIPs and reduces material waste [52].

On the other hand, energy efficiency is another advantage of emulsion polymerization technique. This polymerization technique is usually carried out at lower temperatures, which contributes to energy efficiency and reduces energy consumption. The use of water as a solvent, along with the reduced need for high-temperature reactions, minimizes the overall energy requirements for the synthesis process.

Green electrochemical polymerization as an environmentally-friendly approach utilizes an electrical potential to drive the polymerization reaction during the preparation of green MIPs. It exhibits several advantages that are briefly presented in the following [53].
Green electrochemical polymerization is typically conducted in solvent-free conditions. This eliminates or significantly decrease the use of toxic organic solvents.

This approach based on the application of an electrical potential to initiate and drive the polymerization reaction. By optimizing the applied potential and reaction conditions, the energy consumption can be minimized, contributing to a greener synthesis approach.

Electrochemical polymerization offers control over the polymerization process, By adjusting the applied potential, polymerization time, and functional monomer/template ratio, highly selective green MIPs can be successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The use of green redox initiators
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical polymerization utilizes green redox initiators instead of traditional radical initiators, which are often hazardous. This eliminates the use of toxic and environmentally harmful radical initiators.
Electrochemical polymerization can be efficiently performed in aqueous electrolytes such as phosphate-buffered saline which is non-toxic and readily available.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The use of sustainable electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials (i.e. graphite or carbon nanotubes) which can be effectively obtained from renewable sources or recycled materials are widely employed as sustainable and environmentally-friendly electrode materials in electrochemical polymerization processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmental applications of nanosensors based on green CDs /MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollutants such as pesticides, antibiotics, heavy metals and explosives can have significant negative impacts on the environment and human health [54–58]. Thus, effective and sensitive detection of these pollutants in environmental samples is crucial for effective monitoring, risk assessment, and mitigation strategies [59].

In recent years, many research groups focused on the design and construction of more sensitive and powerful sensor platforms towards various environmental pollutants. Among these, fluoresent nanosensors composed of green CDs anf MIPs received gradually increasing attention due to the many superiorities (i.e. high sensitivity, selectivity, robustness, stability, low-cost, greenness and reusability etc) of these unique materials. In the following, crucial applications of fluoresent nanosensors composed of green CDs anf MIPs are demonstrated.

Pesticides can indeed have the potential to become pollutants when they are not properly used or when their residues accumulate in the environment [60]. It is crucial to emphasize that regulatory authorities establish guidelines and safety measures to mitigate these risks and decrease the negative effects of pesticides. Proper handling of pesticides, adherence to recommended application rates, consideration of environmental factors, and implementation of integrated pest management approaches can help decrease the pesticide pollution and their associated risks. In addition, ongoing scientific studies and progresses in the development of pesticides aim to reduce their environmental impacts and promote more sustainable agricultural practices.

A number of studies on the sensitive detection of various pesticides utilizing fluoresencent nanosensors were performed by the different research groups. Among these, fluoresencent nanosensors based on MIPs and green CDs received gradually increasing attention. Some interesting examples that are reported in the literature are presented in the following:

Acetamiprid, a broad-spectrum insecticide of neonicotinoid type, is potentially dangerous for humans [61]. Thus, it is very crucial to develop an effective and dependable approach for the sensitive detection of acetamiprid or its residues in complex samples including environmental and food samples. The current analytical methods developed for this purpose consist of various gas or liquid chromatographic methods [62], and their combination with mass spectrometry [63,64]. These approaches require sample preparation steps which are time consuming sample, expensive equipment, and expert persons at the laboratory although they are highly accurate and sensitive. In a study performed by Shirani et al. [65], acetamiprid in wastewater and apple samples was sensitively recognized by utilizing a powerful fluorescent nanosensor based on MIPs and green CDs. The synthesis of green CDs was done using citric acid as the carbon source. Then, CDs were doped with silane groups. In the further stage, MIPs were prepared on the surface of silanized CDs by applying sol-gel polymerization apprıach. The prepared fluorescent nanosensor showed high sensing ability for the target compound acetamiprid in real samples. The sensor showed great linearity in terms of recognition response that vary from 7 to 107 nM and the limit of detection was found as 2 nM. The developed label-free approach exhibits prominent superiorities including high selectivity, high sensitivity, label-free and facile preparation compared to the traditional approaches.

Same research group carried out another research [66] on the comparison of MIP/CDs-based sensors and MIP/polymer quantum dots-based sensors in terms of sensing efficiancy for acetamiprid in environmental (sea water, water well, river water and drinking water) and food samples (apple). In the reported research, a novel, highly sensitive and selective optical nanoprobe was successfully fabricated based on coating MIP layer on polymer quantum dots and CDs. The researchers synthesized polymer quantum dots employing a one-step approach using diethylenetriamine and ascorbic acid at room temperature. CDs were also synthesized from the same starting materials through environmetally-friendly hydrothermal apprıoach. In the next step MIPs were prepared based on the previously synthesized CDs and polymer dots. The obtained experimental data illustrated that the utilization MIP/polymer dots-based sensor exhibited a large dynamic concentration range varied between 0.08 and 109 nmol L−1 with a quite lower limit of detection (0.02 nmol L−1). On the other hand, MIP/CDs-based sensor has also great linearity in terms of recognition response that vary from 0.36 to 64 nmol L−1 and the limit of detection was found as 0.11 nmol L−1. Due to the simplicity of the developed approach in this study and all synthesis processes were conducted at room temperature, this approach can be effectively utilized for the recognition of the target pesticides in food and environmental samples. Furthermore, the non-toxic features of MIP and polymer quantum dots can find a wide range of research fields including biological applications.

In another interesting research of the same research group [67], indoxacarb that is a commercially available oxadiazine insecticide was sensitively detected in environmental (i.e. well water) and food samples (i.e. tomato and apple). For this purpose, silica-CDs were firstly synthesized using citric acid as the carbon source. Then, MIP selective towards the target insecticide was prepared on the surface of silica-CDs via sol-gel technique. The linear sensing response of the nanosensor varied in the concentration range between 4 and 102 nM. In addition, quite low limit of detection limit (1 nM) was obtained. The developed MIP/silica-CDs successfully combine the great features of silica-CDs and excellent selectivity of MIP. The other superiorities of this approach compared to the conventional analytical techniques (i.e. GC, HPLC and LC-MS etc.) include its greenness, facile preparation and low cost.

Bhogal et al. carried out a work on the determination of triclosan in environmental water samples by applying the developed green CDs combined with MIPs [68]. For this purpose, hydrothermal approach was utilized for the carbonization of the major constituents of orange juice such as citric acid, fructose, sucrose, glucose and ascorbic which provides the synthesis of CDs. Then, mesoporous silica-based MIP shell was coated on the surface of green CDs. The prepared green nanosensor showed excellent sensing behavior for triclosan in river water, tap water and sewage wastewater. The obtained detection limit was 1.08 nM. This interesting research was the first report on the fabrication and utilization of mesoporous silica-based MIPs coated on the surface of CDs as fluorescence nanoprobes towards triclosan in water samples. The conventional chromatographic approaches are mainly based on the multistep sample preparation processes, time-consuming separation procedures and expensive instrumentation. The developed green approach in this research exhibits a number of attractive features and superiorities including being facile, fast, sensitive and reliable. In addition, the CDs were successfully synthesized from orange juice in a more environmentally-friendly synthetic route to avoid the consumption of hazardous chemicals. It should be also emphasized that this detection approach based on green CDs and MIPs provide an efficient option for the fast and sensitive monitoring of some specific target environmental pollutants such as triclosan due to the electron-transfer induced fluorescence quenching process.

In a published work of Amjadi and Jalili [70], the sensitive recognition of diniconazole, a fungicide belongs to triazole family, in environmental samples including soil, wastewater and river water samples was efficiently performed by applying the fabricated mesoporous MIP/CDs/semiconductor quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor. In the reported study, the synthesis of CDs was carried out by employing environmentally-friendly one-step hydrothermal approach using trisodium citrate as the carbon source. The achieved experimental data confirmed that the fluorescence intensity of the prepared nanosensor is effectively quenched in the existence of diniconazole. The sensor also displayed a linear sensing response towards diniconazole in the concentration range from 20 to 160 μg L−1 with a low limit of detection value (detection limit of 6.4 μg L−1). The main advantage of the developed approach in this study is its sensing mechanism based on the ratiometric measurement. Currently, most of the MIP-coated CDs sensor systems have single wavelength intensity modulation type probes which can be effected by various parameters including the change of concentration, efficiency of the instrument and environmental conditions of the complex samples. These parameters may lead to prevention of the precise detection and determination of the target compound/s. In contrast, the sensing mechanism based on the ratiometric measurement that involves the simultaneous measurement of fluorescence signals at 2 or more wavelengths followed by the determination of the ratio of intensity which can overcome the some disadvantages of these unfavorable effects.

Antibiotics are other crucial pollutants that need to be carefully and sensitively detected in environmental samples. In the following examples, the successful applications of fluorescence nanosensors based on green CDs and MIPs towards various antibiotics as pollutants in real samples are presented.

Chen et al. performed a study on the sensitive determination of an antibiotic sulfadiazine in tap water and milk samples by applying MIP/CDs/CdTe quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor [71]. In the conducted study, sulfadiazine imprinted nanosensor having CDs coated with SiO2 was successfully fabricated through surface imprinting approach using 3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) as the functional monomer (
Fig. 2 a). In their study, the researchers wrapped blue emissive CDs with silica that served as the internal reference signal in order to eliminate background interference. On the other hand, the encapsulation of red emissive CdTe quantum dots modified with thioglycolic acid in the imprinted layer was carried out in order to provide the sensing signal. Figs. 2 b and 2 c show the fluorescence spectra of the imprinted and non-imprinted sensors, respectively in the existence of different concentrations (from 0.25 to 20 μmol L−1) of the target compound sulfadiazine. The developed fluorescence nanosensor showed great linearity in terms of sensing response that vary from 0.25 to 20 µmol L−1. In addition, low limit of detection value (0.042 µmol L−1) was obtained.

In another work [72], Zhang et al. developed photonic crystal MIP array embedded with green CDs for the sensitive recognition of 5 different sulfonamides including sulfadiazine, sulfadimethoxine, sulfaguanidine, sulfathiazole, sulfamethazine in environmental and food samples. The green synthesis of CDs were carried out via hydrothermal technique using citric acid as the carbon source. The results showed that compared to the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs embedded-non-imprinted polymer (NIP) as the control material (ca. 380 nm), the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs embedded-MIPs (ca. 370 nm) exhibited a 10 nm blue shift that can be explained by the change of surface electronegativity of CDs before and after elution of the target sulfonamide. Considering these information, the main form of dynamic fluorescence quenching mechanism of the developed fluorescence nanosensor is photoinduced electron transfer in which the lone pair of electrons from the COOH group on the CDs surface is transferred to the hydrogen bond of the target sulfonamide once the sensing process occurs. This leads to quenching of fluorescence intensity of CDs. In this study, the results also exhibited that the sensing response of the green nanosensor was 200 s. The obtained limit of detection values were in the range from 0.01 to 0.26 nmol L−1.

Sun et al. prepared a fluorescent nanosensor based on green CDs and selective MIPs and utilized for the determination of 5-nitroimidazole antibiotics including metronidazole, secnidazole, ornidazole and tinidazole in river water samples [73]. In this research, catalpa walnut shell was chosen as the green carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. The fluorescence nanosensor was utilized for the efficient sensing of the target antibiotics in the concentration range between 20 and 5000 nM. In the reported research, an environmentally- friendly hydrothermal aproach was successfully employed for the synthesis of green CDs from biomass (walnut shell) without the using of additional toxic chemical reagents. The synthesized green CDs/MIPs nanocomposites effectively combined the great fluorescence features of CDs and the superiorities of the MIPs which can form differential sensing responses to structural analogs.

In a reported study of Wu et al. [74], a MIP/green CDs-based fluorescent nanosensor was fabricated for the sensitive and selective determination of antibiotic norfloxacin in real environmental samples such as lake and tap water. In their study, the researchers successfully synthesized CDs via hydrothermal approach using citric acid as the carbon source. In the further step, SiO2 encapsulated with CdTe quantum dots was prepared. Then, MIPs/CDs/CdTe quantum dots-based nanosensor was prepared via precipitation polymerization approach using methacrylic acid (MAA), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and 2,2- azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) as the functional monomer, cross-linker and initiator, respectively. The developed fluorescence nanosensor showed excellent linearity range in terms of sensing response that vary between 10 and 90 nM. In addition, very low limit of detection value (3.28 nM) was achieved. Furthermore, selectivity and reusability studies confirmed that the fabricated MIP/green CDs-based fluorescent nanosensor can be selectively and efficiently used five times and still retained 93.5 % of the initial sensing behavior that proved the regeneration ability and excellent economic value in practical application of the fluorescent nanosensor.

Luo et al. performed a work on sensitive determination of an antibiotic tetracycline (TC) in aqueous samples [75]. For this purpose, high value-added utilization of wood, a novel environmentally-friendly fluorescent MIP-based hydrogel was developed using wood-derived cellulose nanocrystals (WCNs) from wood and CDs for the sensitive and effective determination of TC. For the synthesis of green CDs, citric acid was chosen as the carbon source and environmentally-friendly hydrothermal approach was utilized. Fig. 3 shows the representation of the preparation of fluorescence nanosensor towards TC in aqueous samples. Under optimized experimental conditions, the obtained results confirmed that the highest binding capacity for TC was found as 544.4 mg g−1 and low detection limit (0.11 µg L−1) was achieved. Furthermore, a cost analysis was also carried for the developed nanosensor and commercially available activated carbon. The obtained data confiröed that the total cost for the efficient sensing and removal 1 g of TC by using the prepared green nanomaterial as almost 34 times cheaper than that of commercially available activated carbon. Considering the achieved all these results, this research could provide an innovative route for the application of wood to design and produce a cheap, sensitive, environmentally-friendly materials for the successfull detection and removal of antibiotics.

Explosive pollutants can be highly dangerous and pose significant risks to human health the environment and infrastructure. Therefore, their sensitive and efficient detection in environmental samples is very crucial [76,77]. A number of research groups developed effective sensing systems for the efficient determination of various explosive pollutants in environmental samples.

For example, 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), mainly used as a powerful explosive, is one of the crucial environmental pollutants. TNP is in the forefront of the list of crucial pollutants in environmental samples because of its high solubility feature in aqueous solutions, low biodegradability and high toxicity. Therefore, the sensitive detection of TNP is very important. For example, in a reported study by Wang et al., TNP in river and tap water samples was sensitively detected by using the prepared magnetic nanosensor based on MIPs and green CDs [78]. In this work, citric acid was chosen as the carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. Then, the preparation of magnetic MIP/CDs was conducted using Fe3O4 nanoparticles. In addition, NIP coated-CDs as the control material were also prepared. Figs. 4 a and 4 b show the fluorescence spectra of the developed magnetic MIP/CDs and NIP/CDs in the existence of various concentrations of TNP in the range between 0 and 100 μM, calibration curve for TNP and dynamic adsorption curve of magnetic MIP/CDs towards TNP. The results exhibited that the prepared magnetic nanosensor based on MIPs and green CDs can be effectively utilized for the sensitive determination of TNP in real environmental samples. As can be seen from the Figs. 4 a and 4 b, the fluorescence intensity of magnetic MIP/CDs compared to the fluorescence intensity of magnetic NIP/CDs decreases more sensitively in the existence of the same concentration of TNP. This result can be attributed to the 3-D cavities in the polymeric structure of MIP created by the target compound TNP during the preparation process of MIP. TNP can also can slightly bind to the magnetic NIP/CDs because of the non-specific binding of TNP on the surface of control polymer NIP/CDs and lead to some fluorescence quenching effect. In addition, magnetic MIP/CDs exhibited good linearity in the range from 1 nM to 100 μM (Fig. 4 c). The sensing response time of the magnetic MIP/CDs towards TNP was also investigated via measuring every 1 min until 60 min. As can be seen from the Fig. 4 d, binding process reached the balance at no more than 1 min which indicated the fast response time towards TNP. In this study, the obtained detection limit value was 0.5 nM.

Same research group carried out another research on the development of a composite fluorescent sensor based on MIP, green CDs and magnetic covalent organic frameworks (COFs) for the efficient sensing of TNP in environmental samples (i.e. rain water, lake water, match stick solution, tap water and dyeing wastewater) [79]. The developed fluorescence sensor displayed great linearity range in terms of sensing response towards TNP which vary from 0.0003 to 100 µM and limit of detection value was achieved as 0.1 nM. The results also displayed that the sensing mechanism of the developed a fluorescent sensor is based on the static fluorescence quenching. One of the superiorities of the developed fluorescent sensor in this study is its rapid sensing response towards the target pollutant TNP. The detection time of TNP was 1 min. Furthermore, this crucial research is the first report on the fabrication of a fluorescent sensor based on MIP, CDs and magnetic COFs.

In another work published by Dai et al. [80], 2,4-dinitrotoluene in aqueous samples was sensitively detected by employing the developed a green nanosensor based on CDs bearing amino groups and MIPs. In the reported work, the authors efficiently synthesized CDs via green approach hydrothermal carbonization method using citric acid as the carbon source. The synthesized CDs were also functionalized with amino groups. In the next step, MIPs/CDs were prepared by using acrylic acid (AA), methyl acrylate (MA) and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) as functional monomers and cross-linker, respectively. The results indicated that the prepared MIPs/CDs-based fluorescence nanosensor exhibited a fast sensing response towards 2,4-dinitrotoluene and has a good linearity range in terms of sensing response that varied from 1 to 15 ppm and the limit of detection was obtained as 0.28 ppm. The prepared fluorescence nanosensor also showed greasy selectivity for the target compound 2,4-dinitrotoluene in the presence of other analogs such as phenol, 4-nitrophenol and dinitrophenol. The obtained data also confirmed that the sensing mechanism of the developed a fluorescent sensor towards 2,4-dinitrotoluene is based on the static fluorescence quenching that may occur as amino-functionalized CDs formed stable charge transfer complex with the target compound 2,4-dinitrotoluene, due to the highly strong electron-withdrawing behavior of nitrated compounds.

Li et al. prepared a fluorescent nanosensor based on MIPs and green CDs for the sensitive recognition of 4-nitrophenol in environmental samples such as tap water and biological samples (i.e. urine) [81]. For this aim, they firstly synthesized reference fluorophore YVO4: Eu3+ nanoparticles. Then, the synthesis of the target sensitive fluorophore green CDs was conducted using citric acid as the carbon source. In the further stage, MIP/CDs with YVO4:Eu3+ were prepared in the existence of 4-nitrophenol in the silica structure through employing sol-gel approach. TEM images of the prepared MIP/CDs having YVO4: Eu3+ nanoparticles and corresponding elemental mapping images of Si, C, N, Y, V, O and Eu, respectively is shown in Fig. 5 . As can be easily seen from the figure, the prepared MIP/CDs having YVO4: Eu3+ nanoparticles exhibit excellent uniform morphology. In addition, the mapping images confirmed that YVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles and CDs are evenly distributed throughout the structure of silica and indicating that both are highly dispersed in the silica particles. Under optimized experimental parameters, the prepared fluorescent nanosensor was effectively utilized for the sensitive recognition of 4-nitrophenol. The selective binding of the target compound 4-nitrophenol to the developed fluorescent nanosensor led to fluorescence quenching of CDs via 2 two possible different routes which are the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between 4-nitrophenol and CDs. The sensor response was linear in the concentration range between 0 and 12.0 μM. The detection limit was obtained as 0.15 μM.

Detection of heavy metal pollutants using fluorescent sensors is a promising and sensitive approach that relies on the interaction between the heavy metal ions and specific fluorescent molecules. These sensor systems offer many advantages such as high sensitivity, real-time detection, and potential for on-site monitoring. In the following examples from the literature, successful applications of fluorescent sensor based on selective MIPs and green CDs are presented.

Wang et al. carried out an interesting work on the development of green CDs modified with ion imprinted polymers and their utilization for the determinations of Cu (II) ions in river and tap water samples [82]. In their study, environmentally-friendly hydrothermal approach and citric acid as the carbon source were employed for the synthesis of green CDs. The obtained data showed that the prepared nanosensor composed of green CD and ion imprinted polymer layer displayed excellent detection ability and sensitivity for the target Cu (II) ions. The obtained detection limit was 2.86 µM and great recoveries of 99.29–105.42% were achieved.

In another research study of Bai et al. [83], the detection of Cu (II) ions was conducted by utilization of dual-functional ion-imprinted polymers doped with CDs. For this aim, they applied environmetally-friendly hydrothermal approach for the preparation of CDs using tryptophan and o-phenylenediamine. Then, Cu (II) imprinted polymer doped with CDs was prepared and effectively employed for the recognition and enrichment of Cu (II) ions in water samples. The developed ion-imprinted polymers doped with CDs displayed great sensing and binding behavior for the target Cu (II) ions in water samples. Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence spectra of prepared ion-imprinted polymers doped with CDs in the existence of various concentrations of Cu (II) ions (from 80 to 780 μmol L−1) and linear response curve curve for the detection of Cu (II) ions. As can be seen from the Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of Cu (II) imprinted polymer doped with CDs continuously reduced with the increase in the concentration of Cu (II) ions in the range between 80 and 780 μmol L−1. There was also a great good linear relationship between the concentration of Cu (II) ions in the range from 80 to 780 μmol L−1 and fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6 b). In this study, the achieved highest binding capacity was 85.6 mg g−1 and the limit of detection was obtained as 3.17 μmol L−1.

Considering the all the achieved results in this work, it can be concluded that the developed environmentally-friendly ion-imprinted polymers doped with CDs are sensitive and powerful materials for the efficient recognition of Cu (II) ions in real environmental samples. It can also be predicted that the design and development of novel MIP/CDs-based fluorescent materials with both selective recognition and fluorescent detection will certainly become a new route for fast detection and removal of various heavy metal pollutants in environmental samples.

Another nanosensor based on ion imprinted polymer and CDs was fabricated by Shao et al. [84]. In the reported research, Li (I) ion imprinted polymer was prepared and combined with green CDs. The researchers used citric acid and environmetally-friendly hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of green CDs. For the preparation of imprinted polymers towards Li (I) ions, methacrylic acid (MAA), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and 2,2- azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were chosen as the functional monomer, cross-linker and initiator, respectively as represented in Fig. 7 a. High selectivity towards the target ion and sensitive fluorescence detection are crucial features of a fluorescent imprinted material. This work also investigated the various interfering ions to determine the selectivity of the prepared.

ion imprinted fluorescence sensor towards Li (I) ions. The obtained selectivity results from these experiments is shown in Fig. 7 b. Under optimized experimental parameters, the prepared nanosensor based on ion imprinted polymer and CDs exhibited high sensing ability and selectivity towards Li (I) ions in lake water samples. The obtained limit of detection was 0.16 μmol L−1.

In a research of Cui et al. [85], rhodamine 6 G in aqueous samples was successfully determined by employing molecularly imprinted mesoporous organosilica encapsulated with green CDs. In this research, the synthesis of CDs was carried out using citric acid as the carbon source. The prepared MIP/CDs-based fluorescent sensor exhibited satisfactory sensing efficiency towards the target dye compound with a linear response in the concentration range between 4 × 10−3 and 7 × 10−3 mg mL−1. In addition, high selectivity is very crucial for MIP-based sensor systems. In this research, the selectivity studies were carried out using rhodamine B and methyl blue as competitive compounds. The obtained results from these studies confirmed that the prepared MIP/CDs-based sensor exhibited high selectivity for rhodamine 6 G. The selective binding mechanism of rhodamine 6 G to the developed fluorescent nanosensor was based on the FRET between rhodamine 6 G and CDs.

Liu et al. published a research on the utilization of CDs-decorated MIPs for the sensitive detection of bisphenol A in environmental water samples [86]. The researchers preferred citric acid as the carbon source for the synthesis of green CDs in their research. The fabricated fluorescent green nanosensor not only displayed a great selective detection behavior derived from MIPs but also exhibited a high sensitivity towards the target contaminant bisphenol A through green CDs. The sensor showed great linearity in terms of recognition response that vary from 10 to 2000 nM and the limit of detection was found as 1.5 nM. The excellent combination of the high specificity of MIPs and the great sensitivity of fluorescence sensing process significantly improved the sensitivity, selectivity and anti-interference capability of the prepared CDs-decorated MIPs-based fluorescence sensor. The fluorescent sensor was efficiently utilized to recognize the level of bisphenol A in real samples. Furthermore, the developed fluorescent sensor offered an excellent approach for the sensitive recognition and rapid identification of various environmental pollutants such as bisphenol A.

Li et al. prepared selective MIP-coated green CDs towards naproxen, a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug, in environmental water and biological samples [87]. The researchers used citric acid as the carbon source and applied hydrothermal technique for the synthesis of CDs in this study. Then, encapsulation of the synthesized CDs into the silica pores via sol-gel approach and selective MIP layer was also formed. After removal of the naproxen from the cross-linked polymeric network, the green nanosensor could selectively detect naproxen in environmental and biological samples with an excellent detection limit (0.03 µM). The obtained linear concentration range of the sensor varied from 0.05 to 4 µM.

MIPs can also be integrated with miniaturized devices such as smartphones [88,89]. Pradup and Thongkon reported the sensitive recognition of nonylphenol, a nonionic surfactant effectively employed in industrial cleaning and household cleaning processes, in soil samples [90]. For this aim, the researchers efficiently synthesized green CDs using citric acid as the carbon source through utilizing the environmentally-friendly microwave irradiation approach. Then, MIPs/CDs were prepared on the surface of cotton fabrics by using the functional monomer 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) monomer and the cross-linker tetraethoxysilane (TEOS). The fluorescence images were obtained and analyzed via smartphone. The fabricated fluorescence nanosensor exhibited satisfactory linearity in terms of recognition response that vary between 100 and 1000 µg L−1 and the achieved limit of detection was 35.9 µg L−1. This interesting research is the first report on the fluorescence sensing based on the MIPs/CDs on the surface of cotton fabrics by using smartphone in order to obtain optical images. The developed approach displayed more portable, flexible and facile operation producing good results that can be compared with the traditional fluorescence spectrometers. Thus, one of the great superiorities of the developed aproach in this research is its on-site applicability outside laboratory for fast screening and quantitative analysis of environmental pollutants.
Sensing of various environmental pollutants by using fluorescence nanosensors based on green CDs and MIPs is given in Table 1.

This review paper provides an overview of green strategies for the synthesis of CDs, their combination of MIPs and latest progresses on the design and construction of powerful and sensitive fluorescent nanosensors based on green CDs and MIPs towards various environmental pollutants. The reported examples demonstrated in this review showed that incorporation of green CDs and MIPs in fluorescent nanosensors significantly enhances the sensitivity and selectivity of the developed nanosensor and provides a great sensing response for the target pollutant/s in environmental samples. Considering the number of superiorities of the green CDs including high solubility in aqueous solutions and photostability, advantages of MIPs such as high affinity, selectivity, low cost, robustness and reusability, fluorescent nanosensors based on green CDs and MIPs are very promising platforms which have great potential in the sensing applications for environmental samples.

Although significant achievements were attained in design and fabrications of novel fluorescent nanosensors based on green CDs and MIPs, there are still substantial design and development challenges and opportunities. Low quantum yields of the produced CDs can lead the limitation for their practical use and large-scale applications. Therefore, innovative synthetic approaches for the efficient synthesis of CDs with high purity and quantum yields should be carefully designed and developed. Furthermore, novel synthesis and surface modification strategies should be developed to improve the biocompatibility and homogeneity of the CDs. On the other hand, the complex polymerization processes for the preparation of MIPs require careful optimization. This can be achieved through taking advantages of molecular simulation technology and simplifying the experimental studies. Studies should also be focused on the design and synthesis of novel functional monomers, cross-linkers, and effective polymerization approaches ro fabricate sensitive and effective sensor platforms.

The efficient surface modification strategies of CDs through doping with electron acceptor or donors, combining to metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs) and nanoparticles should also be investigated to develop multifunctional MIP/CDs-based sensor platforms.

One of the most crucial challenges for sensor applications is the limited sensitivity of the fabricated sensor towards the analyte. Therefore, more effort should be put on the simultaneous improvement of both selectivity sensitivity of the developed novel fluorescent sensors.

The possible integration of CDs and MIPs into the microchips and nanochips should also be considered to design and fabrication of innovative miniaturized devices which is very crucial for on-site applications. Taking the advantage of the fact that MIP/CDs are fully fit with nanotechnology and lab–on–a–chip (LOC) systems. It can certainly be possible to apply these unique materials for both sample preparation and sensing processes on the same platform. This paves the route for novel and powerful miniaturized and portable platforms for on-site detection. Such rapid progresses in sensor platforms are expected to culminate in the design and fabrication of environmentally-friendly, user-friendly, commercially available, highly reliable, selective and sensitive monitoring devices including kits and strips etc. in neat future.
All authors participated equally.
